title: Two Sum
textSolution:
## ✅ HashMap Approach (Optimal)

### Java Code

`java
import java.util.HashMap;

class Solution {
    public int[] twoSum(int[] nums, int target) {
        HashMap<Integer, Integer> map = new HashMap<>();

        for (int i = 0; i < nums.length; i++) {
            int complement = target - nums[i];

            if (map.containsKey(complement)) {
                return new int[]{ map.get(complement), i };
            }

            map.put(nums[i], i);
        }

        return new int[]{-1, -1};
    }
}
`

---

## 🧠 Line-by-Line Explanation

### 1️⃣ Create HashMap

- Key → number from array
- Value → index of that number
- Enables O(1) lookup for the required pair

### 2️⃣ Traverse Array

- Loop through each element once
- Time complexity becomes O(n)

### 3️⃣ Find Complement

- Compute: `target - nums[i]`
- If it exists in map, we found the pair

### 4️⃣ Check Before Storing

- If complement is already in map, return indices
- Prevents using the same element twice

### 5️⃣ Store Current Element

- Add current number and index to map after checking

### 6️⃣ Fallback Return

- Returns `{-1, -1}` for compiler safety

---

## ⏱️ Complexity

- Time: O(n)
- Space: O(n)

---

## 🎯 Interview One-Liner

'I iterate once, compute the complement, check if it exists in a HashMap, and return indices immediately. This reduces time complexity from O(n²) to O(n).'

---

## 🧠 Memory Trick

**Check first, store later**
